coredata.dc:title: Nanobody-based fluorescent immunoassay using carbon dots anchored cobalt oxyhydroxide composite for the sensitive detection of fenitrothion

coredata.dc:description: Fenitrothion (FN) residue in food is a serious threat to public health. Consequently, a sensitive, cost-effective, and convenient immunoassay for FN urgently needs to be fabricated to safeguard human health. Herein, a nanobody-alkaline phosphatase fusion protein (Nb-ALP)-based fluorescent ELISA using red emissive carbon dots (r-CDs) anchored cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheet (CoOOH NS) composite was developed for detecting FN. Briefly, a Nb-ALP was obtained by autoinduction expression and employed as a recognition, signal transduction, and amplification element. As the fluorescence signal source, r-CDs were assembled with CoOOH NS to yield the r-CDs@CoOOH NS composite, leading to the fluorescence quenching of r-CDs via Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). After competitive immunoreaction, the Nb-ALP bounded to the immobilized antigen can mediate the production of ascorbic acid, which can reduce the CoOOH NS to Co2+, breaking the FRET between r-CDs and CoOOH NS, accompanied by the fluorescence recovery of r-CDs. This fluorescent ELISA is highly sensitive to FN with a detection limit of 0.14 ng mL−1, which is 25-fold lower than that of conventional colorimetric ELISAs. The recovery test of food samples and the validation by GC−MS/MS further demonstrated the proposed assay was an ideal tool for detecting FN.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fenitrothion (FN) residue in food is a serious threat to public health. Consequently, a sensitive, cost-effective, and convenient immunoassay for FN urgently needs to be fabricated to safeguard human health. Herein, a nanobody-alkaline phosphatase fusion protein (Nb-ALP)-based fluorescent ELISA using red emissive carbon dots (r-CDs) anchored cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheet (CoOOH NS) composite was developed for detecting FN. Briefly, a Nb-ALP was obtained by autoinduction expression and employed as a recognition, signal transduction, and amplification element. As the fluorescence signal source, r-CDs were assembled with CoOOH NS to yield the r-CDs@CoOOH NS composite, leading to the fluorescence quenching of r-CDs via Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). After competitive immunoreaction, the Nb-ALP bounded to the immobilized antigen can mediate the production of ascorbic acid, which can reduce the CoOOH NS to Co2+, breaking the FRET between r-CDs and CoOOH NS, accompanied by the fluorescence recovery of r-CDs. This fluorescent ELISA is highly sensitive to FN with a detection limit of 0.14 ng mL−1, which is 25-fold lower than that of conventional colorimetric ELISAs. The recovery test of food samples and the validation by GC−MS/MS further demonstrated the proposed assay was an ideal tool for detecting FN.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are extensively applied for pest control in modern agriculture because of their high activity against insects and moderate persistence. Fenitrothion (FN), as one of the most efficient organophosphorus pesticides, is generally utilized to contain pests on cereals, cotton, rice, and vegetables due to its favorable efficiency in the restraint of ﬂies, mosquitoes and cockroaches in farmlands. This application significantly boosts the agricultural productivity and quality (Satnami et al., 2018), but the inappropriate and uncontrolled application of pesticides may extensively contaminate ecosystems and farm products, seriously damaging the ecosystem (Liu and Lin, 2005; Kokulnathan et al., 2021). Additionally, some studies suggested that OP exposure increases the incidences of various illnesses, including diabetes, gestational hypertension, teratogenesis, genotoxicity, and carcinogenesis (Alavanja et al., 2004; Bouchard et al., 2011; Engel et al., 2011). Due to its adverse impacts, the acceptable daily intake (ADI) for FN set by the World Health Organization (WHO) is 0.005 mg/kg (Wu et al., 2019). However, the maximum residue limits (MRLs) of FN in fresh vegetables established by China, the EU and FAO are 0.5 mg kg 1, 0.01 mg kg 1, and 0.5 mg kg 1, respectively (Vinoth Kumar et al., 2018). Therefore, the unceasing monitoring of the FN residue in a wide variety of samples, such as soil, air, water and food, is indispensable. Currently, various analytical methods have been established for detecting FN, such as instrumental analysis (Malhat et al., 2017; Dozein et al., 2021; Schellin et al., 2004; Bazmandegan-Shamili et al., 2018), electrochemistry methods (Özcan et al., 2019; Surucu et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2021), enzyme inhibition methods (Chang et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2021) and immunoassays (Jiang and He, 2011; Jiao et al., 2018; Zou et al., 2019; Cho et al., 2004a). Instrumental methods are sensitive and accurate in FN analysis, but they are expensive and require skilled manpower. Although much progress has been made in constructing highly sensitive and rapid enzyme inhibition-based optical sensors or electrochemical sensors for detecting OPs (Wu et al., 2021; Chang et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2021), these methods are not specific to individual OPs and only yield the total content of OPs. However, these methods cannot specifically determine the content of FN. In comparison, immunoassays have become a promising strategy for pesticide residue monitoring by virtue of their merits of low cost, excellent specificity, easy operation, and high throughput (Yao et al., 2020).

As one of the most commonly used immunoassays, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISAs) have become an effective tool and have been extensively employed for chemical hazard and pesticide detection (Li et al., 2022; Esteve-Turrillas et al., 2018). The majority of current ELISAs for FN were developed by using traditional polyclonal antibodies (pAbs) or monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) (Jiang and He, 2011; Li et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2014; McAdam et al., 1992; Cho et al., 2004b). Specifically, pAbs may suffer from nonspecific recognition, whereas the production of mAbs requires cumbersome cell sorting and a long production cycle, which is expensive and time-consuming. Additionally, the chemical conjugation of enzymes to antibodies in conventional ELISA may result in unstable and randomly cross-linked molecules. Nanobodies (Nbs), also termed as heavy-chain antibodies (VHHs), generally originate from the variable domains of heavy chain antibodies in camelids or sharks (Pillay and Muyldermans, 2021). Nbs have emerged as promising immunodiagnostic reagents for the next generation of immunoassays due to their unique high aqueous solubility, good thermal stability, ease of expression, and genetic modification (Wang and Xianyu, 2022). Thanks to the swift advancements in antibody engineering and the gene recombination techniques, various bifunctional Nb-based fusion proteins, such as Nb-alkaline phosphatase (ALP), Nb-Avi tag, and Nb-streptavidin binding peptide, have been explored for clinical diagnosis, environmental monitoring, and food safety detection (Huo et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2015). Among these fusion proteins, the construction of a Nb-ALP fusion protein is a sought-after strategy for simplifying immunoassays that can avert the chemical coupling of enzymes to antibodies and avoid the use of a secondary antibody (Liu et al., 2015). In addition, the conventional colorimetric readout limits the sensitivity of ELISA, whereas fluorescent ELISAs are generally more sensitive, as demonstrated by many previous studies (Liu et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2018).

Based on our previously prepared anti-FN Nb-jd8 (Chen et al., 2022a), we cloned the Nb-jd8 gene into the ALP gene-containing expression vector pET22b-ALP to prepare the anti-FN Nb-ALP fusion protein. Thus, a highly sensitive fluorescent ELISA was developed based on a novel sensitive fluorescent signal output system via the self-assembly of low-cost nanomaterials. In this system, red-emitting carbon dots (r-CDs) with an emission wavelength of 614 nm were employed as the fluorescence signal source which can rule out spontaneous fluorescence (blue or green emission) from the complex sample matrix; moreover, cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets (CoOOH NSs), as efficient energy receptors, were assembled with r-CDs to form a nanocomposite of r-CDs@CoOOH NSs, which quenched r-CDs through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect. Thus, a highly sensitive fluorescent ELISA for FN was developed by employing the r-CDs@CoOOH NS nanocomposite as the fluorescence signal output module and Nb-ALP as the recognition and signal amplification element ( Scheme 1). As shown in Scheme 1, after a competitive immune reaction, the ALP in Nb-ALP bound to the microplate triggers the dephosphorylation of L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) into ascorbic acid (AA). The resultant AA reduces the CoOOH NSs into Co2+, which destroys the FRET between the r-CDs and CoOOH NSs, resulting in the fluorescence recovery of the r-CDs. Therefore, this system is viable for quantifying FN in a fluorescence-enhancement manner.

Pesticide standards were supplied by Tanmo Technology Ltd. (Beijing, China). 2-Phosphoascorbic acid trisodium salt (AAP), ascorbic acid (AA), cobalt chloride, and N-propylethylenediamine (PSA) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid, tris base, and polysorbate 20 were obtained from Sinopharm Chem. Ltd (Beijing, China). Sodium carbonate, magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), sodium bicarbonate and magnesium chloride hexahydrate were purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Sodium hydroxide, sodium hypochlorite, citric acid and thiourea were obtained from Shanghai Yien Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Gel extraction and PCR purification kits were supplied by TIANGEN (Beijing, China). The primers for cloning the VHH repertoire were synthesized by Invitrogen Biotechnology Co. (Shanghai, China). The E. coli TG1 and E. coli BL21(DE3) were supplied by Weidi Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and TransGen Biotech Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China), respectively. Helper phage M13K07 was supplied by New England Biolabs (MA, USA). The SfI, SacI, and NotI restriction enzymes and T4 DNA ligase were purchased from Thermo Fisher (Shanghai, China). The pComb3xss and pET22b vectors were gifts from Prof. Bruce Hammock (UC Davis, California, USA). Standard solutions of fenitrothion (FN) were purchased from Tan-Mo Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Unfused Nb-jd8 was prepared as described in a previous study (Chen et al., 2022a). Coating antigen (hapten2-BSA) was prepared and stored in our lab (Chen et al., 2021).

Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a SpectraMax i3x multifunction detection platform (Molecular Devices, USA). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption measurements were carried out on an Evolution 300 spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analyses were performed on a Tecnai G2 F20 microscope (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed on an ESCALAB 250XI electron spectrometer (Thermo, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured with a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer. Fourier transform-infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) was conducted using a VERTEX 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried out with a photocounting spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments FLS900, UK). Zeta potentials were measured on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expression and purification of the Nb-ALP fusion protein
ce:section.ce:para: The purified Nb-ALP fusion protein was prepared as previously described (Zhang et al., 2018), except pET22b-ALP, which contains the ALP gene, was used as the expression vector. Briefly, the primers FR1-ALP-SfiI and FR4-ALP-NotI (Table S1) were used to amplify the VHH-jd8 gene from the plasmid jd8-pComb3xss. After digestion with SfiI and NotI, the purified VHH-jd8 gene PCR products were purified again and subcloned into the pET22b-ALP using T4 DNA ligase. The recombinant Nb-ALP plasmid was then transferred into E. coli BL21(DE)3 cells via heat shock at 42 ℃ for 90 s and cultured on LB plates overnight at 37 °C. After sequencing for identification, the positive clone was induced to express Nb-ALP fusion protein by adding isopropyl β-D-1-thiogalactopyranoside (1 mM) to a total of 500 mL of LB culture, and the cells were cultured at 37 ℃ under shaking at 250 rpm for 12 h. The cells were then pelleted by centrifugation at 10000g for 20 min, and Nb-ALP fusion proteins were extracted from the cells using the cold osmotic shock method (Olichon et al., 2007) and purified by Ni-NTA affinity chromatography. The purified Nb-ALP was identified by SDSPAGE, and its concentration was analyzed with a NanoDrop 2000 C system (Thermo Scientific, USA).

The r-CDs were synthesized based on a previously reported method with some modifications (Li et al., 2020). Briefly, 4.0 g of thiourea and 2.0 g of anhydrous citric acid were dissolved in 20 mL of N, N-dimethyl formamide. The mixture was heated at 160 °C for 6 h in a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave. While cooling to room temperature, 40 mL of NaOH solution (50 mg/mL) was added to the mixture under stirring for 5.0 min. The reaction liquid was then dialyzed with ultrapure water for 6 h with a semipermeable membrane (MWCO 800 Da). After dialysis, 2.5 mL of concentrated hydrochloric acid was added and stirred for 10 min followed by a 10 min-centrifugation at 6704g. The obtained precipitate was freeze-dried to yield r-CDs.

The CoOOH NSs were synthesized based on a previously reported method (Liu et al., 2018). Briefly, NaOH solution (1.5 mL, 1.0 M) was mixed with CoCl2 solution (50 mL, 10 mM). The mixture was sonicated for 5 min followed by a 20 min of centrifugation at 3352g. Subsequently, the precipitate was redispersed in 50 mL of distilled water and a portion of sodium hypochlorite solution (2.5 mL, 0.9 mol/L) was added, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 20 min. After that, the mixture was centrifuged at 3352g. for 10 min. The resultant precipitate was washed three times with distilled water.

Preparation of the r-CDs@CoOOH NSs nanocomposite: A total of 10.0 mL of r-CDs aqueous solution (10 μg/mL) was mixed with an equal volume of CoOOH NSs aqueous solution (75 μmol L 1) and sonicated for 30 min. The resultant mixture was centrifuged for 10 min (6704g) followed by washing twice with distilled water. The precipitate was redispersed in distilled water for the further usage.

The microplate was coated with hapten 2-BSA (250 ng/mL, 100 μL/well) for 12 h at 37 °C. After being washed twice with TBST (Tri-HCl buffer, pH 7.4, 5 mmol/L, containing 0.05% Tween-20), the plate was blocked with 120 μL of 5% skim milk powder in ultrapure water (blocking solution) followed by incubation at 37 °C for 3 h. Next, the plate was dried in the oven at 37 ℃ for 1 h, and then stored at 4 ℃ until use.

Construction of the calibration curve proceeded as follows: 50 μL of a series concentrations of FN standard solution (in Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.4, 5 mmol L 1) and 50 μL of Nb-ALP (0.036 μg/mL in Tri-HCl buffer, pH 7.4, 5 mmol L 1) were added to each well, respectively. After incubation at 37 °C for 40 min, the plates were washed five times with TBST, followed by the addition of 100 μL of the AAP solution (2 mmol/L in Tris-HCl, pH 9.7, 5 mmol/L) to each well. After incubation for 30 min at 37 °C, 100 μL of the nanocomposite solution was added to each well. The fluorescence intensity at 614 nm was then measured under the excitation at 554 nm after incubation for 10 min at 37 °C. The calibration curve was obtained by plotting the binding rate ((FFb)/(F0 Fb)) of Nb-ALP in the wells against the logarithm of the FN concentration, where F0 and F correspond to the fluorescence intensity of wells in the absence and presence of FN, respectively; Fb is the background fluorescence intensity of the well when Nb-ALP is absent.

The detection of FN in garden material: 10.0 g sample and 10 mL of acetonitrile were mixed and homogenized in a 50 mL polypropylene centrifuge tube, followed by the addition of magnesium sulfate (4.0 g), sodium chloride (1.0 g), sodium citrate (1.0 g), disodium hydrogen citrate (0.5 g) and a ceramic homogenizer. The mixture was shaken for 1 min, followed by a 5-min centrifugation at 1676g. Subsequently, 6 mL of the supernatant was mixed with MgSO4 (0.9 g) and PSA (0.15 g) and shaken for 1 min, followed by a 5 min-centrifugation at 1676g. The extraction solution was diluted 16-fold in working buffer (Tris-HCl, pH 7.4, 5 mmol/L) for fluorescent ELISA. GC−MS/MS was also employed to validate the fluorescent ELISA. The parameters and conditions of GC−MS/MS are detailed in Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the anti-FN Nb-ALP fusion protein
ce:section.ce:para: In conventional competitive ELISAs for pesticides, enzymes (HRP or ALP) are covalently conjugated to haptens or antibodies via chemical conjugation, which are prone to generate nondirective conjugation products. These products reduce activity of the enzyme or antibody and result in significant batch errors (Chen et al., 2022b; Fu et al., 2020). Nb can address these drawbacks by directionally fusing with ALP via genetic engineering to yield an Nb-ALP fusion protein which simultaneously retains the intact antibody and enzyme activity. Here, we constructed anti-FN Nb-ALP fusion protein via genetic engineering and the purity of the obtained anti-FN Nb-ALP was confirmed by SDS-PAGE. It can be seen from Fig. S1A that a clear band at ~15 kDa and ~65 kDa were observed in the Nb-jd8 lane and Nb-ALP lane, respectively. Moreover, western blotting analysis based on anti-alpaca antibody (Fig. S1B) indicates that the obtained fusion protein contains alpaca-derived nanobody. Matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization time of ﬂight mass spectrometry (MALD-TOF-MS) also was employed to the characterize the anti-FN Nb-ALP. The molecular weight of the anti-FN Nb-ALP is 63.2 kDa (Fig. S2), which matched the molecular weight of a general Nb-ALP fusion protein (Zhang et al., 2018). The activity of anti-FN Nb-ALP was characterized by one-step ELISA using p-nitrophenyl phosphate (pNPP) as substrate. As shown in Fig. S3, the one-step ELISA showed an IC50 of 10.9 ng/mL. These results indicated that an Nb-ALP fusion protein possessing both high antigen-binding affinity and enzymatic activity, illustrating the successful construction of anti-FN Nb-ALP.

Molecular modelling was conducted to study the recognition mechanism between the nanobody and FN (The details of method are presented in Supplementary Materials). Firstly, the three-dimensional (3D) nanobody model (Fig. S4A) was created by SWISS MODEL (http://swissmodel.expasy.org/) (Fu et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. S5A, the CDR1/2/3 regions of Nb-jd8 are all coil structures connected with many beta-sheets, which confers a high ﬂexibility, especially to the CDR1 region. In turns, CDR1 ﬂexibility alters the binding pocket formed with CDR2/3 during the molecular recognition. The Ramachandran plot in Fig. S4B indicates 97.9% and 2.1% of the residues in the most favored and additionally allowed region, respectively, manifesting the reasonable conformation of the Nb-jd8 model. Docking simulations between Nb-jd8 and FN were performed using LeadIT 2.3.2 software (http://www.biosolvet.de/LeadIT/) via Enthalpy and Entropy (hybrid approach) ligand binding setting. As shown in Fig. S4C and D, a deep docking pocket is formed for the complete inserting of FN molecule. The 2D and 3D docking results (Fig. S4E and F) illustrated that the nitro group of FN inserted to the bottom of docking pocket and formed a 1.3 Å hydrogen bond to Tyr37 (CDR1). Moreover, the docking pocket consisting of Ala33 (CDR1), Leu47(FR2), Tyr104 and Lys98 (CDR3) formed a hydrophobic interaction with the benzene rings of FN. The high affinity of Nb-jd8 to FN also might be benefited from the hydrophobic interaction between the phosphorothioate and Gly50, Ile51 of CDR2, as well as a 1.9 Å hydrogen bond formed between Tyr104 and the oxygen atom of phosphorothioate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of r-CDs@CoOOH NS nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: As a precursor for CoOOH Ns, CoCl2 exhibits a characteristic adsorption peak at 510 nm). In comparison, the CoOOH NSs display an intense absorption from 350 nm to 700 nm and possess a characteristic absorption peak at 425 nm (Fig. S5). Therefore, CoOOH NSs can be used as an efficient quencher to many fluorescent donors, including the r-CDs used in this work. The TEM image ( Fig. 1 A) displays the typical hexagon nanosheet morphology of CoOOH, and the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) clearly revealed a crystal lattice fringe of 0.226 nm (inset of Fig. 1 A), which matched the d-spacing of the (012) plane of CoOOH (Lee et al., 2012). The XRD pattern in Fig. 1B exhibited three typical peaks at 20.0°, 39.2° and 50.6°, which could be matched well with the (003), (012) and (018) peaks, respectively, according to the standard JCPDS card (No. 07–0169), illustrating the successful synthesis of crystalline CoOOH (Liu et al., 2018). Fig. 1 C shows the FT-IR spectrum of the CoOOH NSs. The broad peak located at ~3300 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of the hydrogen-bonded hydroxyl group (−OH). The peak centered at ~1652 cm−1 was assigned to the Co−O double bond in the crystal structure of CoOOH. The peak at 579 cm−1 is matched with the characteristic peak of the Co−O2− complex vibration (Li et al., 2019). Additionally, the CoOOH NS solution exhibited good colloid stability for 2 h without any precipitation (Fig. S6). These results demonstrate that the successful preparation of CoOOH NSs.

The as-synthesized r-CDs were also characterized by TEM and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The TEM image indicates that r-CDs were uniformly dispersed with average diameters of 3.0 ± 0.2 nm (Fig. 1D and E). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 1D reveals the high crystallinity of the r-CDs whose lattice spacing was 0.21 nm. Furthermore, XPS measurements were used to investigate the valence state of the elements and composition of the r-CDs. Fig. 1 F displays the full-range XPS of r-CDs, indicating that r-CDs mainly contain sulfur, carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. As shown in the high-resolution C 1 s spectra of r-CDs (Fig. 1 G), the binding energy peaks at 286.3 eV, 287.4 eV and 290 eV were attributed to the graphitic sp2 carbon, CN and NCO structures of the r-CDs, respectively. The high resolution XPS characterization of O 1 s showed three peaks at 531.8 eV, 532.6 eV and 535.4 eV (Fig. 1H), which belong to CO, COC and COH, respectively (Li et al., 2020). For N 1 s, the three peaks in the N 1 s region were ascribed to pyridinic N (399.5 eV), pyrrolic N (400.5 eV) and graphitic N (401.8 eV), respectively (Fig. 1I). These results suggested that the r-CDs might have a pyridine or pyrrolic conjugation core, which contributed to the long wavelength fluorescence emission performance.

The r-CDs@CoOOH composite consisting of CoOOH NSs and r-CDs was facilely fabricated via mixing and sonication. The zeta potentials of the r-CDs, CoOOH NSs and r-CDs@CoOOH NS nanocomposites were 29.9 mV, + 8.45 mV and 21.6 mV, respectively ( Fig. 2 A), indicating that the assembly of r-CDs@CoOOH NS composite occurred via electrostatic interactions. The TEM image of the mixed system of r-CDs and CoOOH NSs reveals that the r-CDs were well interspersed on CoOOH NSs (Fig. 2B). In the r-CDs@CoOOH NS nanocomposites, CoOOH NSs acted as an acceptor to efficiently quench the fluorescence intensity of r-CDs. overlap between the absorbance spectrum of CoOOH NSs and the fluorescence spectrum of r-CDs, which is a prerequisite for the FRET between r-CDs and CoOOH NSs. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity of r-CDs progressively declined as the concentration of CoOOH NSs gradually increased from 0 to 100 μmol L−1. When 75 μmol L−1 of CoOOH NSs were incubated with r-CDs, the fluorescence quenching efficiency was approximately 90% (inset of Fig. S7), which can be attributed to the exceptional light absorption capability and large superficial area of CoOOH NSs. In addition, the lifetime of r-CDs decreased from 3.3 ns to 2.4 ns after assembly with CoOOH NSs (Fig. 2D), further suggesting that the quenching mechanism was due to the FRET process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the r-CDs@CoOOH NS nanocomposite-based fluorescent signal output system
ce:section.ce:para: Given their strong oxidative capacity, CoOOH NSs can reportedly be reduced into Co2+ by AA (Zhang et al., 2021). Therefore, the fluorescence of r-CDs can be restored when adding AA to the r-CDs@CoOOH NS nanocomposite solution. Fig S8 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the r-CDs@CoOOH NS nanocomposite solution in the presence of different concentrations of AA. It can be seen that characteristic absorption peak at 425 nm of CoOOH NSs gradually decreased to disappearance with the increase of the AA concentration. Moreover, the TEM indicates that after the addition of AA, the typical hexagon nanosheet morphology of CoOOH NSs cannot be found (Fig. S9). Additionally, the XRD spectrum of CoOOH NSs after incubation with AA shows that typical peaks of CoOOH disappeared, whereas new peaks appeared at 20.25°, 23.04°, 29.4°, 32.67°, and 34.98° can be well indexed to ascorbic acid (JCPDS No.22–1536) (Fig. S10). These results confirmed that the crystalline structure of the CoOOH NSs were damaged and degraded by AA. Furthermore, XPS measurement has been conducted for CoOOH NSs before and after incubation with AA. It can be seen in Fig S11 that after incubation with AA, the peak of Co 2p on the full-range XPS spectra significantly decreased, and high-resolution Co 2p3/2 spectra can be fitted into three peaks including 781, 782.7 and 785.6 eV, which is greatly different from the Co 2p3/2 spectra of CoOOH NSs (780, 781 eV). The XPS measurement reveals the variation of cobalt oxidation state from Co (III) to Co (II) in the reaction between CoOOH NSs and AA, further verifying CoOOH NSs can be reduced to Co2+ by AA. Nevertheless, AA and Co2+ do not markedly change the fluorescence of r-CDs, i.e., neither of these components interfere with the system (Fig. S12). To optimize the fluorescence response to AA, the working concentration of CoOOH NSs and reaction time were adjusted. As shown in Fig. S13, the system has the optimal fluorescence response to AA when the concentration of CoOOH NSs was 75 μmol L−1 with 5 min response time. Under the optimal condition, the fluorescence intensity of the r-CDs@CoOOH NSs system progressively recovered as the AA concentration increased, and a sensitive AA concentration-dependent fluorescence response was achieved at the low concentration range of 0.1–1 μmol L−1 ( Fig. 3 A and B).

As a typical enzyme used in ELISA, ALP can efficiently catalyze the dephosphorylation of AAP to AA, thus reducing the r-CDs@CoOOH NSs nanocomposite and restoring the fluorescence of r-CDs. Fig. 3 C shows that the fluorescence intensity of the r-CDs@CoOOH NS nanocomposite cannot be restored in the presence of only ALP. Although AAP can recover the fluorescence of r-CDs@CoOOH NS nanocomposite to some extent, it can be seen that AA possesses much higher fluorescence recovery ability to the r-CD@CoOOH NSs than AAP does (Fig. S14A). As a result, the fluorescence intensity can be further significantly enhanced with the addition of ALP and the production of AA (Fig. 3 C). The fluorescence recovery by AAP can be ascribed to the fact that the r-CDs anchored on the surface of CoOOH NSs can be replaced with AAP due to the strong electrostatic attraction between AAP and CoOOH NSs; thus, the FRET efficiency between the dissociated r-CDs and CoOOH NSs decreases accompanied by the fluorescence enhancement of the system (The details of the deduction were presented in Supplementary Materials). As displayed in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence intensity of the system continuously increases as the ALP activity concentration increases from 0 to 5 mU mL−1. A linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the logarithm value of ALP activity concentrations was observed at low concentration range from 0.2 to 3 mU mL−1, R2 = 0.995. The limit of quantification for ALP is comparable to that of the commercial AttoPhos AP fluorescent substrate system (linear range: 0.1–10 mU mL 1, Fig. S15), and lower than or comparable to those of most reported strategies (Table S3), indicating the high response sensitivity of this system to ALP activity. These data lay a solid foundation for constructing a highly sensitive fluorescent ELISA.

The r-CDs@CoOOH NS nanocomposite with highly sensitive fluorescent response to ALP and AA inspires us to apply it to a fluorescent ELISA for detecting FN. To attain the optimal detection performance of Nb-ALP-mediated fluorescent ELISA for FN, the concentration of coating antigen and Nb-ALP, which are two critical factors that strongly influence the assay sensitivity were optimized. Initially, checkerboard titration was employed to screen the optimal coating antigen and Nb-ALP concentration combinations based on the competitive inhibition rate (IR), which were calculated according to the following equation: IR= (1 −F/F0) × 100%, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the wells in the absence and presence of FN (10 ng mL 1), respectively. Three coating antigen and Nb-ALP concentration combinations (125 ng mL 1 and 36 ng mL 1, 250 ng mL 1 and 36 ng mL 1, and 500 ng mL 1 and 18 ng mL 1) maximizing the IR were selected to construct calibration curves. As shown in Fig.S16 A, B and C, the fluorescence intensity of the reaction solution exhibited a gradual decrease as the FN concentration increased. The best detection sensitivity (half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of 2.19 ng mL−1) was achieved, when the concentrations of coating antigen and Nb-ALP were 250 ng mL 1 and 36 ng mL 1, respectively (Fig. S16D). At this optimal coating antigen and Nb-ALP concentration combination, the limit of detection (LOD, defined as IC10) for FN was 0.14 ng mL−1, which is ~25-fold lower than that of conventional colorimetric ELISA (IC10 of 3.6 ng mL 1, Fig. S3) using the same Nb-ALP. Compared with previously constructed biotinylated Nb-based fluoro-immunoassay involving multiple reagent-addition and incubation steps (Chen et al., 2022a), this fluorescent ELISA adopting direct competitive format is advantageous because it is simple to operate. Nonetheless, the as-developed fluorescent ELISA has a higher or comparable sensitivity than most of previously reported immunoassays for FN (Table S4). The high sensitivity is attributed to signal amplification because of the use of the r-CDs@CoOOH NS nanocomposite as a fluorescent signal generation system. For conventional fluorogenic systems, short wavelength fluorescent signals (blue or green emission) are commonly employed for quantification, which are prone to interference by spontaneous fluorescence from a complex sample matrix, resulting in a relatively high background signal (Li et al., 2020). The strong light absorption capability of the CoOOH NSs makes them excellent quenchers on r-CDs via FRET, achieving a low-background or background-free fluorescent detection. Thus, a fluorescent ELISA with high signal-to-noise (S/N ratio) was developed.

To investigate the specificity of the fluorescent ELISA on FN, some OPs sharing a similar structure with FN and some common pesticides were employed as cross-reactants. As shown in Table 1, only low cross-reactivities (CRs) to parathion-methyl (18.9%) and parathion (1.9%) were observed. The CRs with the other six pesticides were less than 0.1%, indicating the satisfactory specificity of the fluorescent ELISA.

More importantly, the sample matrix effect should be considered for practical application. Suppressing the matrix effect to an acceptable level is essential before analysis. As shown in the sample pretreatment procedures ( Fig. 4 A), the sample extract solution was diluted with working buffer after the extraction and purification steps to further reduce the sample matrix effect. Specifically, the matrix effect of orange and cabbage decreased to an acceptable level when their sample extract solutions were subjected to a 16-fold dilution in working buffer (Fig. 4B and C). Therefore, the LOD of this method for orange and cabbage samples is 2.24 μg/kg, which is lower than the strictest MRL for FN (0.01 mg kg 1). As a result, orange and cabbage samples were spiked with 40, 80, and 160 ng mL−1 with FN standard. The spiked samples were then successively subjected to the extraction by acetonitrile, purification by MgSO4 and PSA, and a 16-fold dilution in working buffer to exclude background interference before being analyzed by the fluorescent ELISA. Table 2 shows that recoveries ranging from 84.5% to 116% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) of less than 12.1% were obtained. The reliability of the fluorescent ELISA was validated by GCMS/MS. The results from these two methods are consistent (Table 2), suggesting the promising accuracy and potential practicability of the fluorescent ELISA.

In summary, we have constructed an Nb-ALP fusion protein and r-CDs@CoOOH NS composite to fabricate a sensitive and reliable fluorescent ELISA for FN detection. By harnessing the efficient enzymatic reaction and exceptional optical properties of r-CDs@CoOOH NSs, this fluorescent ELISA display a notable advantage in terms of sensitivity, with a 25-fold improvement in sensitivity compared with conventional colorimetric ELISA. Moreover, based on the Nb-ALP fusion protein, the fluorescent ELISA was performed in a direct competitive format, which simplified the detection procedure by avoiding the use of secondary or tertiary antibodies. Notably, this fluorescent ELISA platform not only avoids complex chemical modification and conjugation procedures but also effectively circumvents the use of costly commercial fluorescent substrates. In conclusion, this work offers a powerful strategy for detecting FN and other toxic analytes in an efficient, sensitive, and cost-effective manner.

Fenitrothion is a kind of broad-spectrum organophosphate insecticide has been extensively utilized to prevent, control, or eliminate pests on crops, highly boosting the productivity in modern agriculture. However, excessive use and improper use of pesticide also cause widespread contamination of natural water, soil, air, and farm products, even severely breakdown ecosystems. The sensitive analysis of pesticide is of great significance to satisfy the demand for food safety, to protect the ecosystem and to safeguard human health. This work renders a potentially powerful strategy for detecting fenitrothion or other toxic analytes in an efficient, sensitive, and cost-effective fashion.
